caption:
Stop writing if/elif chains in Python.

match/case destructures, guards, and matches types — all in one expression. It's not a switch statement. It's structural pattern matching.

Python 3.10+. Which pattern do you use? A or B?

#python #patternmatching #coding #programming #developer #backend #pythontips #cleancode #python310 #codingtips

slides.title: Which branching is cleaner?
slides.problem: Handle multiple cases with complex conditions

a.label: if / elif

b.label: match / case
b.code:
def handle(event):
  match event:
    case {'type': 'click',
          'pos': [x, y]}:
      process_click(x, y)
    case {'type': 'key',
          'mod': 'ctrl',
          'key': k}:
      shortcut(k)

slides.title: B wins. Destructures + matches in one step.

verdicts.title: Manual key access, nested ifs
verdicts.text: Every condition is a new line. Miss a key and you get KeyError at runtime. Doesn't compose.

verdicts.title: Pattern matching extracts AND checks
verdicts.text: Destructures values and checks structure simultaneously. Cleaner, safer, and the linter catches missing patterns.

slides.takeaway: match/case is not a switch statement. It's structural pattern matching.

slides.title: It does way more than switch.
slides.sub: Type matching. Guards. Nested destructuring. Wildcards.
slides.code:
match command:
  # Match by type
  case str(s):
    print(f'String: {s}')
  case int(n) if n > 0:
    print(f'Positive: {n}')
  # Match nested structures
  case {'user': {'name': str(name),
                 'age': int(a)}} if a >= 18:
    grant_access(name)
  # Wildcard fallback
  case _:
    print('Unknown')

items.text: match value: case pattern: — basic matching
case int(n) if n > 0: — type + guard
